narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 stood out as the most influential factor in the model's decision, as it slightly raised the predicted risk of failing the bar exam on the first try—despite being a bit higher than the 3.24 average among students who passed. This edged out your Year 3 school decile ranking of 9th, which worked strongly in your favor by suggesting solid performance relative to peers and pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your LSAT score of 40 also played a key role, though it nudged the odds a little higher toward failure compared to the typical passer’s 37.27 average. Overall, these three elements—your undergrad grades, later-year ranking, and LSAT—were the biggest drivers, with your GPA tipping the balance just enough against you. The model estimated a 59.6% chance you'll fail the bar exam first time around, which is why your full-time law school application was rejected; the university uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass on their initial attempt. Your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who pass, and your strong Year 1 decile of 9 further supports your potential, but it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. Compared to typical accepted students, who often have undergrad GPAs a touch lower but balanced by even stronger rankings or test scores, your profile was close but crossed into higher-risk territory here. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid LSAT and academic deciles showing real promise. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher or bolstering your undergrad record through post-bac courses or additional certifications that highlight improvement. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing GPA perceptions head-on in reapplications. Many with profiles like yours have turned rejections into acceptances elsewhere by showing proactive steps. Keep building on your strengths—your deciles and income background position you well for another strong shot.
